title: Raising awareness of the racial disparities in the impact of colorectal cancer on the Hispanic community.
plain_summary: This bill is about a type of cancer called colorectal cancer that affects Hispanic people more than other groups. Hispanic people are less likely to get tested for this cancer and more likely to die from it. The House of Representatives wants to make people aware of this problem and asks government agencies to study why this happens. They also want these agencies to find ways to help Hispanic communities get better cancer testing and treatment.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution is about raising awareness of how colorectal cancer (cancer of the colon and rectum) affects Hispanic people more than other racial groups.

sections.title: Background Facts
sections.text: Colorectal cancer is the second most deadly cancer for Hispanic men and the third most deadly for Hispanic women. Hispanic people are less likely to get tested for this cancer than other groups. Only forty nine percent of Hispanic adults who should get tested actually do, compared to fifty eight percent of white adults. Testing can find cancer early when it is easier to treat, and sometimes testing can prevent cancer by removing growths that could become cancer later. Health experts say people should start getting tested at age forty five if they have normal risk. By the year two thousand thirty, this cancer is expected to become the top cause of death for people between ages twenty and forty nine. Better awareness, education, and access to testing and treatment are important for reducing this cancer, especially in communities that do not have good access to healthcare. March is National Colorectal Cancer Awareness Month, which gives a chance to teach people about risk factors, warning signs, and ways to prevent this disease.

sections.title: House of Representatives Actions
sections.text: The House of Representatives agrees to take these actions. First, it recognizes that colorectal cancer has a deadly impact on American people. Second, it supports National Colorectal Cancer Awareness Month. Third, it acknowledges that Hispanic people face unfair differences in how this cancer affects them. Fourth, it encourages the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (the government agency that tracks diseases) to continue and expand its work to find out why Hispanic people get tested less often and to develop ways to fix this problem. Fifth, it encourages people to learn about their risk for colorectal cancer and talk with their doctors about getting tested. Sixth, it encourages the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention to study environmental factors that might cause this cancer, and the National Institutes of Health (the government medical research agency) to study what causes young adults to have higher risk for this cancer. Seventh, it honors the courage of patients, survivors, caregivers, and advocates who are affected by colorectal cancer.
